column3: Cardiac output = heart rate × stroke volume.
column4: Falls with severe bradycardia, tachycardia or reduced stroke volume; compensation raises heart rate and systemic vascular resistance.
column5: A normal blood pressure can coexist with reduced output if vasoconstriction is strong.
column6: Use the equation to interpret shock, exercise and drug effects.

column3: MAP is approximated by diastolic pressure + one third of pulse pressure at normal heart rates; physiologically MAP ≈ cardiac output × systemic vascular resistance.
column4: Hypotension may reflect reduced output, reduced vascular resistance or both.
column5: Do not equate systolic pressure alone with organ perfusion.
column6: Separate pump failure from vasodilatory shock using output and resistance.

column3: Within physiological limits, greater end-diastolic fibre stretch increases force of contraction and stroke volume.
column4: A fluid-responsive patient increases stroke volume when preload rises; a failing ventricle may sit on the flat part of the curve.
column5: Frank–Starling changes force through preload, not intrinsic contractility.
column6: Predict the response to a fluid challenge or venous-return change.

column3: Preload reflects ventricular end-diastolic stretch and is influenced by venous return, blood volume and ventricular compliance.
column4: It falls in haemorrhage and rises with fluid loading or venoconstriction.
column5: Filling pressure is only a surrogate and can be high despite low effective filling in a stiff ventricle.
column6: Interpret JVP, fluid responsiveness and pressure–volume loops.

column3: Afterload is the load against which the ventricle ejects, closely related to arterial pressure, vascular resistance and wall stress.
column4: Raised afterload reduces stroke volume and increases myocardial oxygen demand.
column5: Systemic vascular resistance is not identical to afterload, particularly with valvular obstruction.
column6: Explain why vasodilators improve forward flow in selected heart-failure states.

column3: Contractility is intrinsic myocardial force generation at a given preload and afterload.
column4: Catecholamines increase contractility; myocardial infarction and severe acidosis reduce it.
column5: A larger stroke volume after fluid does not by itself prove increased contractility.
column6: Distinguish inotropy from preload effects on a pressure–volume loop.

column3: Ejection fraction = stroke volume ÷ end-diastolic volume.
column4: It is reduced in many systolic-failure states but may be preserved or high when the ventricle is small or concentric.
column5: A normal EF does not guarantee normal stroke volume or normal diastolic function.
column6: Interpret HFrEF, HFpEF and low-flow states.

column3: Increased preload raises end-diastolic volume and widens the pressure–volume loop if contractility and afterload are unchanged.
column4: Stroke volume increases through Frank–Starling physiology.
column5: End-systolic pressure–volume relation does not shift with isolated preload change.
column6: Recognise the loop change after fluid loading.

column3: Increased afterload raises systolic pressure, increases end-systolic volume and narrows the loop.
column4: Stroke volume falls and myocardial work rises.
column5: Do not mistake the higher pressure for improved contractility.
column6: Recognise acute hypertension or aortic stenosis effects.

column3: Increased contractility steepens the end-systolic pressure–volume relation and reduces end-systolic volume.
column4: Stroke volume and ejection fraction increase at the same preload.
column5: It is distinguished from preload increase by a leftward end-systolic point.
column6: Recognise catecholamine or inotrope effects.

column3: S1 is produced mainly by mitral and tricuspid valve closure at the start of systole.
column4: It is loud with short PR or mobile stenotic mitral leaflets and soft with long PR or severe mitral regurgitation.
column5: S1 is not semilunar valve opening.
column6: Link timing of murmurs to the cardiac cycle.

column3: Inspiration delays pulmonic valve closure because increased right-sided filling prolongs right ventricular ejection.
column4: A2 precedes P2 and the split widens on inspiration.
column5: Expiration narrows the normal split; it does not remain fixed.
column6: Distinguish normal splitting from ASD, RBBB and LBBB patterns.

column3: An atrial septal defect produces relatively fixed right ventricular volume loading across respiration.
column4: S2 remains widely split during inspiration and expiration.
column5: Do not confuse with wide variable splitting from RBBB.
column6: Use the sound pattern to identify ASD physiology.

column3: Delayed right ventricular emptying, classically RBBB or pulmonary stenosis, delays P2.
column4: The split is present at expiration and widens further with inspiration.
column5: Unlike ASD, respiratory variation remains.
column6: Differentiate conduction delay from fixed shunt physiology.

column3: Delayed left ventricular emptying causes A2 to occur after P2.
column4: The split appears on expiration and narrows or disappears on inspiration.
column5: Think LBBB or severe aortic stenosis, not RBBB.
column6: Localise delayed semilunar valve closure.

column3: The a wave reflects right atrial contraction immediately before ventricular systole.
column4: It is absent in atrial fibrillation and prominent when right ventricular filling is resisted.
column5: It is not the carotid upstroke and is not palpable.
column6: Use waveform timing to identify rhythm and tricuspid inflow obstruction.

column3: Intermittent atrial contraction against a closed tricuspid valve creates cannon a waves.
column4: Seen with AV dissociation, complete heart block or ventricular tachycardia.
column5: Regular giant a waves suggest fixed resistance; irregular cannon waves suggest variable AV timing.
column6: Recognise AV dissociation at the bedside.

column3: The v wave reflects right atrial filling during ventricular systole.
column4: Large systolic v waves occur in significant tricuspid regurgitation.
column5: The v wave rises during systole, unlike the presystolic a wave.
column6: Link waveform timing to valvular lesions.

column3: JVP rises or fails to fall during inspiration because the right ventricle cannot accommodate increased venous return.
column4: Occurs in constrictive pericarditis, restrictive cardiomyopathy and right ventricular infarction.
column5: Cardiac tamponade usually causes prominent x descent but classically not Kussmaul sign.
column6: Differentiate impaired right-sided filling states.

column3: Exaggerated inspiratory fall in systolic pressure reflects ventricular interdependence and reduced left ventricular filling.
column4: Classically seen in cardiac tamponade and severe asthma.
column5: It is a quantitative inspiratory pressure change, not disappearance of the pulse by definition.
column6: Use respiratory haemodynamics to identify tamponade or severe airflow obstruction.

column3: Reduced carotid sinus and aortic arch stretch decreases afferent firing, increasing sympathetic and reducing vagal output.
column4: Standing causes tachycardia, vasoconstriction and increased contractility.
column5: The reflex is rapid; RAAS and renal sodium retention are slower.
column6: Explain orthostatic responses and reflex tachycardia.

column3: Reduced renal perfusion, reduced macula-densa sodium chloride or beta-1 stimulation releases renin, producing angiotensin II and aldosterone.
column4: Angiotensin II constricts efferent arterioles and aldosterone retains sodium while excreting potassium and hydrogen ions.
column5: ADH retains water but is not part of the renin–angiotensin enzymatic cascade.
column6: Interpret heart failure, renal artery stenosis and hyperaldosteronism.

column3: Most left coronary flow occurs in diastole because systolic myocardial compression limits intramural flow.
column4: Tachycardia shortens diastole and can provoke ischaemia.
column5: Right coronary flow is less diastole-dominant because right ventricular systolic pressure is lower.
column6: Explain demand ischaemia during tachyarrhythmia.

column3: Demand rises with heart rate, contractility and ventricular wall stress.
column4: Beta-blockade lowers demand; hypertension and tachycardia increase it.
column5: Coronary oxygen extraction is already high, so increased demand is met mainly by increased flow.
column6: Explain anti-anginal drug effects.

column3: Wall stress increases with intracavitary pressure and chamber radius and decreases with wall thickness.
column4: A dilated ventricle has greater wall stress and oxygen demand; concentric hypertrophy initially reduces stress.
column5: Hypertrophy may reduce stress but worsen compliance and oxygen supply–demand balance.
column6: Link ventricular geometry to heart failure.

column3: Low circulating volume reduces preload, stroke volume and cardiac output; sympathetic tone raises heart rate and systemic vascular resistance.
column4: Typical profile: low filling pressures, low output, high resistance and cool peripheries.
column5: Early blood pressure may be maintained despite significant volume loss.
column6: Distinguish volume loss from distributive shock.

column3: Primary pump failure reduces cardiac output while filling pressures and systemic vascular resistance rise.
column4: Pulmonary oedema, cool peripheries and raised JVP may coexist.
column5: Fluids may worsen congestion when the ventricle is failing.
column6: Interpret post-MI hypotension and haemodynamic profiles.

column3: Pathological vasodilation lowers systemic vascular resistance; cardiac output is often high early and may fall later.
column4: Warm peripheries and wide pulse pressure can occur in early sepsis.
column5: A normal or raised output does not mean tissue oxygen delivery is adequate.
column6: Differentiate vasoplegia from pump failure.

column3: Mechanical obstruction limits filling or outflow, reducing cardiac output despite preserved intrinsic myocardial function.
column4: Tamponade, tension pneumothorax and massive pulmonary embolism cause raised venous pressure with hypotension.
column5: The obstruction site determines whether lungs are clear or congested.
column6: Identify the reversible mechanical cause from bedside findings.

column3: The P wave represents atrial depolarisation.
column4: Abnormal morphology can suggest atrial enlargement or ectopic atrial activation.
column5: Atrial repolarisation is usually hidden within the QRS complex.
column6: Use P-wave presence and relation to QRS to analyse rhythm.

column3: The PR interval reflects atrial-to-ventricular conduction, mostly AV nodal delay.
column4: It lengthens in first-degree AV block and shortens with accessory-pathway pre-excitation.
column5: PR segment depression is a different finding, associated with pericarditis.
column6: Localise conduction delay or pre-excitation.

column3: The QRS complex represents ventricular depolarisation.
column4: Widening suggests bundle-branch delay, ventricular origin, pre-excitation or toxic/metabolic conduction slowing.
column5: It does not represent ventricular contraction itself.
column6: Distinguish supraventricular from ventricular rhythm mechanisms.

column3: QT spans ventricular depolarisation through repolarisation and varies with heart rate.
column4: Prolongation increases torsades risk; shortening occurs with hypercalcaemia.
column5: Use a corrected QT rather than comparing raw QT at very different rates.
column6: Assess electrolyte and drug-related arrhythmic risk.

column3: Spontaneous phase-4 depolarisation is driven by funny current, calcium currents and declining potassium conductance.
column4: Sympathetic stimulation steepens phase 4 and increases rate; vagal stimulation flattens it.
column5: Pacemaker cells lack the stable resting potential of ventricular myocytes.
column6: Explain autonomic effects on heart rate.

column3: Rapid sodium influx produces phase-0 depolarisation in ventricular myocytes and His–Purkinje tissue.
column4: Sodium-channel blockade slows conduction and can widen QRS.
column5: AV nodal phase 0 depends mainly on calcium, not fast sodium channels.
column6: Predict tissue-specific antiarrhythmic effects.

column3: The plateau reflects balanced L-type calcium influx and potassium efflux.
column4: Calcium entry triggers calcium-induced calcium release and contraction.
column5: It is absent as a prominent plateau in nodal pacemaker cells.
column6: Connect electrical activity to excitation–contraction coupling.

column3: Raised extracellular potassium initially accelerates repolarisation, then depolarises resting potential and inactivates sodium channels.
column4: Progression may include peaked T waves, PR prolongation, QRS widening and sine-wave morphology.
column5: ECG severity does not correlate perfectly with serum potassium, so a near-normal ECG does not exclude danger.
column6: Explain why calcium stabilises myocardium without lowering potassium.

column3: Low extracellular potassium delays repolarisation and promotes triggered activity.
column4: ST depression, flattened T waves, prominent U waves and apparent QT/QU prolongation may occur.
column5: Prominent U waves can be mistaken for a second T wave.
column6: Link diuretic loss to ventricular arrhythmia risk.

column3: Hypercalcaemia shortens the plateau and QT interval; hypocalcaemia prolongs QT mainly by prolonging ST.
column4: Severe hypocalcaemia can predispose to ventricular arrhythmia.
column5: Calcium disorders alter QT more than QRS width.
column6: Use ECG duration to support electrolyte interpretation.

column3: Low magnesium facilitates early afterdepolarisations in a prolonged repolarisation state.
column4: It increases torsades risk, especially with QT-prolonging drugs or hypokalaemia.
column5: IV magnesium treats torsades even if serum magnesium is not markedly low.
column6: Recognise the electrophysiological rationale for magnesium therapy.

column3: Septal depolarisation and injury are represented mainly by V1–V2; proximal LAD septal branches supply the interventricular septum.
column4: ST elevation in V1–V2 suggests septal/anterior injury in the right context.
column5: V1–V2 reciprocal ST depression can instead indicate posterior infarction.
column6: Combine lead direction with reciprocal changes and clinical context.

column3: The anterior left ventricle is represented mainly by V3–V4 and is commonly supplied by the LAD.
column4: ST elevation in V3–V4 localises anterior injury.
column5: Do not assume every anterior-pattern elevation is occlusion; consider early repolarisation, LVH and pericarditis.
column6: Link contiguous leads to the likely myocardial territory.

column3: Proximal LAD occlusion may produce ST elevation across V1–V6 with I and aVL involvement.
column4: Large anterior infarcts carry risk of pump failure, ventricular arrhythmia and septal/free-wall complications.
column5: The exact artery cannot be determined solely from a generic anterior label.
column6: Use lead extent and complications to infer proximal disease.

column3: I, aVL, V5 and V6 view the lateral left ventricle, supplied commonly by circumflex or diagonal branches.
column4: ST elevation in these contiguous leads suggests lateral injury.
column5: Isolated high-lateral change in I/aVL may be subtle and may have inferior reciprocal depression.
column6: Distinguish lateral from anterior and inferior patterns.

column3: II, III and aVF view the inferior wall, usually supplied by the RCA in right-dominant circulation and sometimes the circumflex.
column4: Inferior ST elevation may accompany bradycardia, AV block or right ventricular involvement.
column5: Inferior location does not automatically prove RCA occlusion.
column6: Use reciprocal and right-sided leads to refine the culprit artery.

column1: RCA versus circumflex in inferior STEMI
column3: RCA occlusion is favoured when ST elevation in III exceeds II, with reciprocal depression in aVL and possible right-ventricular changes.
column4: Circumflex occlusion is more likely with lateral involvement and relatively greater elevation in II.
column5: These are probabilistic ECG clues, not absolute angiographic proof.
column6: Apply multiple lead relationships rather than a single rule.

column3: Proximal RCA occlusion can infarct the right ventricle; right-sided lead V4R is the key additional lead.
column4: Hypotension, raised JVP and clear lungs suggest preload-dependent RV failure.
column5: Nitrates can worsen hypotension by reducing preload.
column6: Recognise the haemodynamic and ECG combination.

column3: Posterior injury produces reciprocal anterior changes: horizontal ST depression, tall R waves and upright T waves in V1–V3; V7–V9 may show ST elevation.
column4: Often accompanies inferior or lateral infarction from RCA or circumflex disease.
column5: Do not dismiss isolated anterior ST depression as simple subendocardial ischaemia without considering posterior occlusion.
column6: Use posterior leads to reveal the hidden injury vector.

column1: Left main or multivessel ischaemia pattern
column3: Diffuse ST depression with ST elevation in aVR can reflect global subendocardial ischaemia from left main, proximal LAD or severe multivessel disease.
column4: It is a high-risk pattern in the appropriate acute syndrome.
column5: It is not specific enough to diagnose left-main occlusion by itself.
column6: Recognise global supply–demand or proximal coronary danger.

column3: Biphasic or deeply inverted T waves in V2–V4 during a pain-free period can indicate critical proximal LAD disease.
column4: Cardiac markers may be normal or only mildly raised despite high short-term risk.
column5: Exercise testing is unsafe because it may precipitate infarction.
column6: Identify a reperfusion-pattern warning rather than benign T-wave inversion.

column3: Upsloping ST depression at the J point with tall symmetrical precordial T waves and possible aVR elevation can indicate proximal LAD occlusion.
column4: It is an occlusion-equivalent pattern requiring urgent recognition.
column5: Do not label it simply as hyperkalaemia without considering regional lead distribution and symptoms.
column6: Recognise an anterior occlusion without classic ST elevation.

column3: Dominance is defined by the artery giving rise to the posterior descending artery, usually the RCA.
column4: It influences inferior/posterior territory and AV-nodal blood supply.
column5: Dominance is not determined by which coronary artery is physically larger.
column6: Explain variation in infarct patterns and complications.

column3: The AV nodal artery usually arises from the RCA in right-dominant circulation and from the circumflex in left dominance.
column4: Inferior MI can cause transient AV nodal block and bradycardia.
column5: High-grade block in anterior MI more often reflects extensive His–Purkinje necrosis and has worse prognosis.
column6: Use block location to infer infarct territory and severity.

column3: The posteromedial papillary muscle has a single main blood supply, usually from the posterior descending artery, making it more vulnerable after inferior MI.
column4: Rupture causes acute severe mitral regurgitation, pulmonary oedema and shock.
column5: A new murmur may be soft when forward output is very low.
column6: Link post-MI timing and arterial supply to mechanical complication.

column3: Transmural septal necrosis can rupture, creating an acute left-to-right shunt.
column4: A harsh new murmur, shock and step-up in right-sided oxygen saturation may occur.
column5: The haemodynamic lesion is a shunt, unlike papillary rupture causing regurgitation.
column6: Differentiate mechanical complications after MI.

column3: Transmural necrosis can rupture into the pericardial space, producing haemopericardium and tamponade.
column4: Sudden collapse, electromechanical dissociation or recurrent pain may occur days after MI.
column5: It does not produce the prominent left-to-right shunt of septal rupture.
column6: Identify catastrophic post-infarct tamponade.

column3: Alveolar ventilation = respiratory rate × (tidal volume − dead-space volume); arterial CO2 varies inversely with alveolar ventilation.
column4: Shallow rapid breathing may give poor ventilation despite a high respiratory rate.
column5: Minute ventilation includes dead-space ventilation and may be misleading.
column6: Explain hypercapnia from reduced effective ventilation.

column3: V/Q mismatch occurs when ventilation and perfusion are unevenly matched across lung units.
column4: It is the commonest mechanism of hypoxaemia and usually improves with supplemental oxygen.
column5: A true shunt responds poorly to oxygen compared with ordinary V/Q mismatch.
column6: Interpret hypoxaemia in COPD, asthma, pneumonia and pulmonary embolism.

column3: Perfusion of non-ventilated lung or anatomical right-to-left flow mixes deoxygenated blood with systemic arterial blood.
column4: Severe pneumonia, pulmonary oedema and ARDS can cause shunt-like hypoxaemia.
column5: High inspired oxygen has limited effect when blood bypasses ventilated alveoli.
column6: Distinguish shunt from low V/Q and hypoventilation.

column3: Dead space is ventilation that does not participate effectively in gas exchange; physiological dead space includes anatomical and alveolar components.
column4: Pulmonary embolism increases alveolar dead space by ventilating unperfused lung.
column5: Dead space is not the same as shunt; one lacks perfusion, the other lacks ventilation.
column6: Explain tachypnoea and inefficient ventilation in PE.

column3: The A–a gradient compares calculated alveolar oxygen with measured arterial oxygen.
column4: It is normal in pure hypoventilation or low inspired oxygen and raised in V/Q mismatch, diffusion impairment or shunt.
column5: The normal gradient increases with age and inspired oxygen.
column6: Use it to classify mechanisms of hypoxaemia.

column3: Gas transfer falls when membrane thickness increases, surface area decreases or capillary transit time is too short.
column4: Interstitial lung disease lowers diffusion capacity and may cause exertional desaturation.
column5: Oxygen is normally perfusion-limited at rest but can become diffusion-limited in disease.
column6: Link DLCO and exercise hypoxaemia to alveolar-capillary pathology.

column3: Low alveolar oxygen constricts nearby pulmonary arterioles, diverting blood toward better-ventilated units.
column4: It improves local V/Q matching but widespread hypoxia raises pulmonary artery pressure.
column5: This is opposite to systemic vascular responses to hypoxia.
column6: Explain pulmonary hypertension in chronic lung disease and altitude.

column3: Type 1 respiratory failure is hypoxaemia without hypercapnia, usually from V/Q mismatch, shunt or diffusion failure.
column4: PaO2 is low while PaCO2 is normal or low because ventilation is maintained or increased.
column5: It is a gas-exchange failure, not necessarily a ventilation failure.
column6: Differentiate pneumonia/PE physiology from ventilatory failure.

column3: Type 2 respiratory failure includes hypercapnia from inadequate alveolar ventilation, usually with hypoxaemia.
column4: Causes include severe COPD, neuromuscular weakness, central depression and chest-wall failure.
column5: Hypercapnia requires insufficient effective ventilation; V/Q mismatch alone is not enough.
column6: Identify when ventilatory support is required.

column3: Increased CO2, hydrogen ions, temperature and 2,3-BPG reduce haemoglobin oxygen affinity.
column4: A right shift promotes oxygen unloading in metabolically active tissue.
column5: Affinity falls even though PaO2 may be unchanged.
column6: Apply the Bohr effect in fever, acidosis and exercise.

column3: Reduced CO2, alkalosis, lower temperature, fetal haemoglobin and carbon monoxide increase apparent oxygen affinity.
column4: A left shift impairs tissue unloading despite high saturation.
column5: Carbon monoxide also reduces available binding sites, so normal PaO2 is misleading.
column6: Interpret fetal oxygen transfer and carbon-monoxide poisoning.

column3: Raised CO2 and hydrogen ions decrease haemoglobin affinity for oxygen, facilitating peripheral unloading.
column4: Actively metabolising tissues extract oxygen more effectively.
column5: The Bohr effect concerns CO2/H+ effects on oxygen binding; the Haldane effect concerns oxygenation effects on CO2 carriage.
column6: Distinguish the two named gas-transport effects.

column3: Deoxygenated haemoglobin binds more CO2 and hydrogen ions; oxygenation in the lungs promotes CO2 release.
column4: High-concentration oxygen can reduce haemoglobin CO2 carriage and contribute to rising PaCO2 in susceptible COPD.
column5: It is not simply loss of hypoxic respiratory drive.
column6: Explain one component of oxygen-induced hypercapnia.

column3: High oxygen can worsen V/Q mismatch by reversing hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction and can increase dissolved CO2 through the Haldane effect.
column4: PaCO2 may rise rapidly in patients with chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure.
column5: Reduced respiratory drive is usually not the sole or dominant explanation.
column6: Justify controlled oxygen targets and repeat blood gases.

column3: Compliance is change in volume per change in pressure; it rises in emphysema and falls in fibrosis, oedema and ARDS.
column4: Low compliance increases work of breathing; high compliance impairs elastic recoil.
column5: Easy inflation in emphysema does not mean effective ventilation.
column6: Interpret pressure–volume curves and ventilator pressures.

column3: Type II pneumocytes produce surfactant, reducing surface tension, increasing compliance and preventing alveolar collapse.
column4: Deficiency causes neonatal respiratory distress; dysfunction contributes to atelectasis.
column5: Surfactant has greatest stabilising effect in smaller alveoli.
column6: Apply Laplace law to alveolar stability.

column3: For a sphere, pressure needed to keep it open is proportional to surface tension and inversely proportional to radius.
column4: Without surfactant, small alveoli would empty into larger alveoli and collapse.
column5: Surfactant is not merely a lubricant; it changes surface tension dynamically.
column6: Explain atelectasis and neonatal distress.

column3: Airflow obstruction disproportionately lowers FEV1, reducing FEV1/FVC.
column4: Air trapping may increase residual volume and total lung capacity.
column5: A low FVC alone does not prove restriction because severe obstruction can trap air.
column6: Recognise asthma/COPD patterns.

column3: Restriction reduces lung volumes with preserved or increased FEV1/FVC; total lung capacity confirms restriction.
column4: Fibrosis produces low TLC and often low DLCO.
column5: Spirometry alone cannot definitively diagnose restriction without lung-volume measurement.
column6: Separate true restriction from poor effort or air trapping.

column3: A significant post-bronchodilator rise in airflow supports variable reversible obstruction.
column4: It is common in asthma but may also occur in COPD.
column5: Absence of reversibility on one test does not exclude asthma.
column6: Interpret spirometry in context rather than using a single absolute rule.

column3: DLCO reflects alveolar-capillary surface area, membrane conductance and pulmonary capillary blood volume.
column4: It falls in emphysema, interstitial lung disease, pulmonary vascular disease and anaemia; it may be normal or high in asthma.
column5: Anaemia lowers DLCO without primary lung pathology.
column6: Use DLCO to distinguish causes of obstruction or restriction.

column3: Central chemoreceptors respond mainly to CSF hydrogen ions generated from CO2; carotid bodies respond rapidly to low PaO2, pH and PaCO2.
column4: Peripheral hypoxic drive becomes prominent when PaO2 falls substantially.
column5: Central chemoreceptors do not directly sense arterial oxygen.
column6: Explain ventilatory responses to hypercapnia, hypoxia and metabolic acidosis.

column3: Occluded perfusion creates high V/Q and dead space; redistribution can also create low-V/Q regions.
column4: Hypoxaemia with respiratory alkalosis is common, but normal oxygen does not exclude PE.
column5: The primary lesion is perfusion failure, not airway obstruction.
column6: Connect clot burden to dead space, tachypnoea and right-heart strain.

column3: When expiratory time is insufficient, incomplete exhalation causes progressive air trapping and intrinsic PEEP.
column4: Occurs in severe asthma/COPD and can impair venous return, causing hypotension during ventilation.
column5: Increasing respiratory rate can worsen rather than improve ventilation.
column6: Recognise the need for longer expiratory time.

column3: Closing capacity is the lung volume at which small dependent airways begin to close; it rises with age and supine posture.
column4: When it exceeds FRC, dependent airway closure and hypoxaemia become more likely.
column5: Obesity, anaesthesia and pregnancy reduce FRC and magnify the problem.
column6: Explain peri-operative and age-related desaturation.

column3: Diffuse inflammatory alveolar-capillary injury causes permeability oedema, surfactant dysfunction, low compliance and intrapulmonary shunt.
column4: Hypoxaemia is often refractory to simple oxygen and may improve with PEEP/proning.
column5: This is non-cardiogenic oedema, though cardiac and ARDS mechanisms can coexist.
column6: Link severe systemic insult to shunt physiology.

column3: GFR depends on filtration coefficient and net filtration pressure across glomerular capillaries.
column4: It falls with low renal perfusion, obstruction or reduced filtration surface.
column5: Serum creatinine changes lag behind an acute fall in GFR.
column6: Interpret AKI before equilibrium is reached.

column3: Afferent constriction reduces renal blood flow and glomerular capillary pressure, lowering GFR.
column4: NSAID-mediated prostaglandin inhibition can precipitate this in volume-depleted patients.
column5: Both renal blood flow and filtration pressure fall.
column6: Explain haemodynamic AKI with NSAIDs.

column3: Moderate efferent constriction raises glomerular pressure and filtration fraction despite reduced renal blood flow.
column4: Angiotensin II helps preserve GFR during reduced perfusion.
column5: Severe constriction can eventually lower GFR by reducing flow and increasing oncotic pressure.
column6: Understand RAAS compensation in hypovolaemia.

column3: ACE inhibition dilates the efferent arteriole and lowers intraglomerular pressure.
column4: Creatinine may rise after initiation, especially with bilateral renal artery stenosis or severe volume depletion.
column5: A modest stable rise is not automatically drug toxicity.
column6: Recognise haemodynamic renal failure and when to investigate.

column3: Filtration fraction = GFR ÷ renal plasma flow.
column4: It rises with moderate efferent constriction and falls with afferent constriction or efferent dilation.
column5: GFR and renal plasma flow can change in different directions.
column6: Use paired changes to localise arteriolar effects.

column3: The proximal tubule reabsorbs most filtered sodium and water, nearly all glucose/amino acids, and most bicarbonate iso-osmotically.
column4: Carbonic anhydrase inhibition causes bicarbonaturia and proximal acidosis.
column5: Water follows solute, so tubular fluid remains roughly iso-osmotic.
column6: Localise Fanconi syndrome and acetazolamide effects.

column3: The NKCC2 transporter reabsorbs sodium, potassium and chloride; the segment is impermeable to water and creates medullary hypertonicity.
column4: Loop diuretics impair concentrating ability and increase calcium/magnesium excretion.
column5: It dilutes tubular fluid despite reabsorbing solute.
column6: Explain loop-diuretic electrolyte effects.

column3: The NCC transporter reabsorbs sodium chloride; the segment participates in calcium handling under PTH influence.
column4: Thiazides reduce urinary calcium and can cause hyponatraemia and hypokalaemia.
column5: This contrasts with loop diuretics, which increase calcium loss.
column6: Differentiate Gitelman-like from Bartter-like physiology.

column3: Principal cells reabsorb sodium through ENaC and secrete potassium; intercalated cells regulate acid–base balance.
column4: Aldosterone increases ENaC activity and potassium/hydrogen loss; ADH increases water permeability via aquaporin-2.
column5: Aldosterone and ADH have distinct primary actions.
column6: Interpret hyperaldosteronism, amiloride and DI.

column3: Active salt reabsorption in the water-impermeable ascending limb and water exit from the descending limb build the medullary osmotic gradient.
column4: The vasa recta preserve the gradient by countercurrent exchange.
column5: ADH uses the gradient but does not create it alone.
column6: Explain urine concentration and loop-diuretic effects.

column3: ADH binds V2 receptors on collecting-duct principal cells, inserting aquaporin-2 channels into the apical membrane.
column4: Water is reabsorbed down the medullary osmotic gradient, concentrating urine.
column5: V1 receptors mediate vasoconstriction, not collecting-duct water permeability.
column6: Differentiate central DI, nephrogenic DI and SIADH.

column3: Inappropriate ADH causes water retention, low serum osmolality and inappropriately concentrated urine with continued urinary sodium loss after mild volume expansion.
column4: Patients are usually clinically euvolaemic rather than overtly oedematous.
column5: A concentrated urine in hypotonic hyponatraemia is abnormal unless a non-osmotic ADH stimulus exists.
column6: Interpret paired serum and urine osmolality/sodium.

column3: Absent ADH secretion or renal ADH resistance prevents urine concentration, producing polyuria and dilute urine.
column4: Central DI responds to desmopressin; nephrogenic DI does not substantially concentrate.
column5: Primary polydipsia also gives dilute urine but suppresses ADH appropriately.
column6: Use water deprivation and desmopressin response conceptually.

column3: FeNa estimates the fraction of filtered sodium excreted.
column4: A low value can support avid sodium retention in pre-renal physiology; a higher value may support tubular injury.
column5: Diuretics, CKD, pigment injury and early obstruction can make FeNa misleading.
column6: Use as supportive evidence, not a standalone AKI diagnosis.

column3: FeUrea may be less affected than FeNa by loop or thiazide diuretics.
column4: A low value can support pre-renal physiology in a diuretic-treated patient.
column5: It is still imperfect in sepsis, CKD and mixed renal injury.
column6: Choose the more appropriate fractional-excretion index.

column3: Anion gap = sodium − (chloride + bicarbonate), representing unmeasured anions.
column4: It rises with lactate, ketones, renal acids and selected toxins.
column5: Low albumin lowers the expected gap and can mask an anion-gap acidosis.
column6: Classify metabolic acidosis before finding the cause.

column3: The rise in anion gap compared with the fall in bicarbonate helps detect a second metabolic process.
column4: A disproportionately small bicarbonate fall suggests concurrent metabolic alkalosis; a large fall suggests additional normal-gap acidosis.
column5: It is useful only after accounting for baseline/albumin and clinical context.
column6: Identify mixed metabolic disorders.

column3: Expected PaCO2 in metabolic acidosis is approximately 1.5 × bicarbonate + 8, with a small range.
column4: Measured PaCO2 above expected indicates additional respiratory acidosis; below expected indicates respiratory alkalosis.
column5: Compensation does not normally overcorrect to alkalosis.
column6: Detect mixed respiratory pathology in DKA or sepsis.

column3: Hypoventilation raises PaCO2 in response to metabolic alkalosis, but compensation is limited by hypoxaemia.
column4: PaCO2 rises roughly in proportion to the bicarbonate increase.
column5: Marked hypercapnia should prompt a superimposed ventilatory disorder.
column6: Identify mixed alkalosis and respiratory failure.

column3: Bicarbonate loss or impaired acid excretion is offset by chloride retention, preserving the anion gap.
column4: Diarrhoea, renal tubular acidosis and saline loading are common mechanisms.
column5: A normal gap does not mean the acidosis is mild or benign.
column6: Separate gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss from renal acidification failure.

column3: Type 1 RTA is failure of distal hydrogen-ion secretion, causing normal-gap acidosis with inappropriately high urine pH.
column4: Hypokalaemia and calcium-phosphate stones/nephrocalcinosis may occur.
column5: Urine pH is less useful after systemic acidosis has corrected.
column6: Recognise stone-forming hypokalaemic RTA.

column3: Type 2 RTA is impaired proximal bicarbonate reabsorption.
column4: Urine is initially alkaline during bicarbonate wasting but can become acidic once serum bicarbonate falls below the new threshold.
column5: A persistently high urine pH is more characteristic of distal RTA.
column6: Link to Fanconi syndrome and carbonic anhydrase inhibition.

column3: Hypoaldosteronism or aldosterone resistance reduces potassium and hydrogen secretion.
column4: Produces hyperkalaemic normal-gap metabolic acidosis, often in diabetic kidney disease or with RAAS-blocking drugs.
column5: Hyperkalaemia distinguishes it from most type 1/2 RTA presentations.
column6: Recognise the aldosterone-dependent pattern.

column3: Effective measured osmolality is driven mainly by sodium salts, glucose and urea; tonicity excludes freely permeable ineffective osmoles such as urea.
column4: Hyperglycaemia can raise measured osmolality and pull water extracellularly.
column5: A high urea raises osmolality but contributes less to sustained transcellular water shift.
column6: Distinguish osmolality from tonicity.

column3: Marked extracellular glucose draws water from cells, lowering measured sodium without total-body hypotonicity to the same degree.
column4: Corrected sodium may be normal or high in hyperglycaemic crises.
column5: This is not pseudohyponatraemia from laboratory measurement artefact.
column6: Interpret sodium in DKA/HHS.

column3: Low effective arterial volume, pain, nausea and stress can stimulate ADH despite low plasma osmolality.
column4: A hypovolaemic patient may retain water and produce concentrated urine even when hyponatraemic.
column5: This is physiologically appropriate ADH, unlike SIADH.
column6: Use volume status to interpret urine concentration.

column3: Insulin stimulates Na/K-ATPase, moving potassium into cells without removing it from the body.
column4: It rapidly lowers serum potassium in hyperkalaemia when given with glucose as needed.
column5: The effect is redistribution; definitive potassium removal requires renal or gastrointestinal elimination/dialysis.
column6: Distinguish temporising from definitive therapy.

column3: Beta-2 stimulation increases cellular potassium uptake through Na/K-ATPase activation.
column4: Nebulised salbutamol can lower serum potassium but response is variable.
column5: Non-selective beta-blockade can impair this shift.
column6: Explain adjunctive hyperkalaemia treatment.

column3: Mineral metabolic acidosis tends to shift potassium out of cells, while organic acidoses have more variable effects; renal handling and insulin deficiency also matter.
column4: DKA may present with high serum potassium despite severe total-body depletion.
column5: Do not assume every acidosis causes the same potassium shift.
column6: Interpret potassium before and during insulin treatment.

column3: PTH raises serum calcium by increasing renal calcium reabsorption, phosphate excretion and activation of vitamin D, and by promoting bone resorption indirectly.
column4: Primary hyperparathyroidism gives high calcium with low phosphate in typical renal function.
column5: PTH does not directly increase renal phosphate reabsorption.
column6: Interpret calcium, phosphate and PTH patterns.

column3: Calcitriol increases intestinal calcium and phosphate absorption and supports bone mineral balance.
column4: Deficiency causes secondary hyperparathyroidism and defective mineralisation.
column5: Unlike PTH, vitamin D tends to increase phosphate availability.
column6: Distinguish vitamin-D deficiency from primary hyperparathyroidism.

column3: Volume and chloride depletion maintain alkalosis through increased proximal bicarbonate reabsorption and aldosterone activity.
column4: Vomiting or remote diuretic use usually gives low urine chloride and improves with chloride/volume replacement.
column5: Active diuretic use may keep urine chloride high despite volume depletion.
column6: Use urine chloride to classify alkalosis.

column3: Hypothalamic TRH stimulates pituitary TSH, which stimulates thyroid T4/T3; circulating thyroid hormone suppresses TRH and TSH.
column4: Primary hypothyroidism gives low free T4 with high TSH; primary hyperthyroidism gives high hormone with suppressed TSH.
column5: Pituitary disease can produce an inappropriately normal rather than frankly low TSH.
column6: Localise primary versus central thyroid disease.

column3: Pituitary or hypothalamic failure produces low free T4 with low or inappropriately normal TSH.
column4: Other pituitary hormone deficits may coexist.
column5: A TSH within the laboratory range is not reassuring when free T4 is low.
column6: Avoid diagnosing primary thyroid disease from TSH alone.

column3: CRH stimulates ACTH, which stimulates cortisol; cortisol suppresses CRH and ACTH.
column4: Primary adrenal failure gives low cortisol with high ACTH; secondary failure gives low cortisol with low/inappropriately normal ACTH.
column5: Aldosterone is mainly regulated by RAAS and potassium, so it is relatively preserved in secondary adrenal failure.
column6: Localise adrenal versus pituitary disease.

column3: Dexamethasone should suppress pituitary ACTH and endogenous cortisol in normal physiology.
column4: Failure of low-dose suppression supports autonomous Cushing physiology; further testing localises the source.
column5: Exogenous dexamethasone is designed not to be measured as cortisol in standard assays.
column6: Understand the feedback principle rather than memorising one isolated cut-off.

column3: Primary hyperaldosteronism suppresses renin; secondary aldosterone elevation occurs with high renin from reduced effective perfusion.
column4: Both can cause hypertension, hypokalaemia and metabolic alkalosis, but renin separates the mechanisms.
column5: Drug effects and sodium status can distort the ratio.
column6: Interpret adrenal versus renovascular physiology.

column3: Endogenous proinsulin cleavage releases insulin and C-peptide in equimolar amounts; injected insulin lacks C-peptide.
column4: High insulin with low C-peptide suggests exogenous insulin, while both high suggests endogenous secretion or sulfonylurea exposure.
column5: Renal failure can elevate C-peptide through reduced clearance.
column6: Investigate unexplained hypoglycaemia.

column3: Insulin deficiency plus counter-regulatory hormones drives lipolysis, ketogenesis, hyperglycaemia, osmotic diuresis and total-body electrolyte loss.
column4: Serum potassium may be normal/high initially despite total-body depletion and falls with insulin.
column5: Closing the anion gap/ketosis matters more than glucose normalisation alone.
column6: Explain fluid, insulin and potassium sequencing.

column3: Severe hyperglycaemia causes profound osmotic diuresis and hyperosmolality with enough insulin to limit major ketogenesis.
column4: Neurological impairment correlates with osmolality and dehydration.
column5: Some ketosis or acidosis can coexist, especially in mixed DKA/HHS.
column6: Differentiate dominant hyperosmolar from ketoacidotic physiology.

column3: Hypothalamic dopamine tonically inhibits prolactin secretion.
column4: Dopamine antagonists and pituitary-stalk disruption raise prolactin; high prolactin suppresses GnRH.
column5: Prolactin is unusual because its dominant hypothalamic control is inhibitory.
column6: Explain galactorrhoea, amenorrhoea and antipsychotic effects.

column3: Growth hormone is pulsatile and stimulates hepatic/tissue IGF-1, which mediates many growth effects and provides feedback.
column4: IGF-1 is a more stable screening marker than a random GH measurement.
column5: Oral glucose normally suppresses GH; failure supports acromegaly physiology.
column6: Choose appropriate dynamic endocrine testing.

column3: FSH recruits follicles; granulosa aromatase produces oestradiol under FSH influence while theca cells generate androgen under LH.
column4: Rising oestradiol initially suppresses gonadotropins and proliferates endometrium.
column5: The two-cell, two-gonadotropin model explains why both cell types are needed.
column6: Interpret ovarian-cycle hormone patterns.

column3: Sustained high oestradiol switches to positive feedback, generating the mid-cycle LH surge.
column4: The surge triggers ovulation and luteinisation.
column5: A single oestradiol rise is not ovulation itself.
column6: Link hormonal feedback to timing of fertility.

column3: The corpus luteum secretes progesterone, converting endometrium to a secretory state and raising basal body temperature.
column4: Without pregnancy, luteolysis lowers progesterone and menstruation follows.
column5: FSH is not the dominant luteal hormone.
column6: Interpret cycle timing and progesterone testing.

column3: Loss of ovarian follicles lowers oestradiol/inhibin and removes negative feedback, raising FSH and LH.
column4: FSH typically rises more prominently than LH.
column5: An isolated normal FSH does not exclude perimenopause because secretion fluctuates.
column6: Explain vasomotor symptoms and laboratory patterns.

column3: HbF binds 2,3-BPG less strongly than adult haemoglobin, shifting its oxygen curve left.
column4: This supports placental oxygen transfer from maternal to fetal blood.
column5: Higher affinity aids loading but reduces unloading; fetal circulation compensates with high output and haemoglobin concentration.
column6: Explain maternal–fetal oxygen exchange.

column3: Ductus venosus bypasses much of the liver, foramen ovale directs IVC blood RA→LA, and ductus arteriosus directs pulmonary artery flow to aorta.
column4: High pulmonary resistance and low placental resistance maintain the pattern before birth.
column5: The umbilical vein is oxygenated; umbilical arteries are deoxygenated.
column6: Trace fetal blood through all three shunts.

column3: Lung expansion lowers pulmonary resistance while cord clamping raises systemic resistance; left atrial pressure exceeds right and functionally closes the foramen ovale.
column4: Increased oxygen and reduced prostaglandins promote ductus arteriosus constriction.
column5: Anatomical closure follows functional closure and may be delayed.
column6: Explain persistent fetal circulation and duct-dependent lesions.

column3: Maternal and fetal blood do not normally mix directly; gases diffuse across the placental barrier along partial-pressure gradients.
column4: Double Bohr/Haldane effects aid transfer as maternal blood releases oxygen and fetal blood releases CO2.
column5: Placental exchange depends on maternal and fetal perfusion as well as diffusion.
column6: Interpret fetal compromise in placental insufficiency.

column3: Cervical stretch increases oxytocin, strengthening uterine contraction and further stretch until delivery.
column4: This is a classic positive-feedback loop.
column5: Most endocrine axes use negative feedback; labour is an exception.
column6: Explain progression of labour.

column3: Prolactin drives milk synthesis; oxytocin causes myoepithelial contraction and milk ejection.
column4: Suckling reduces dopamine and triggers oxytocin release.
column5: Prolactin and oxytocin have different roles; one makes milk, the other releases it.
column6: Interpret postpartum lactation failure and drug effects.

column3: Arterial oxygen content depends mainly on haemoglobin concentration × saturation, with a small dissolved oxygen component.
column4: Severe anaemia can cause low oxygen content despite normal PaO2 and saturation.
column5: PaO2 measures dissolved oxygen tension, not total carriage.
column6: Explain tissue hypoxia with normal pulse oximetry.

column3: Oxygen delivery = cardiac output × arterial oxygen content.
column4: Shock, anaemia and hypoxaemia can each reduce delivery through different terms.
column5: Normal saturation does not compensate for critically low output or haemoglobin.
column6: Integrate haemodynamics with gas carriage.

column3: CO binds haemoglobin with high affinity, reducing available sites and left-shifting remaining oxygen binding.
column4: PaO2 may be normal and conventional pulse oximetry may appear reassuring.
column5: The key abnormality is oxygen content and unloading, not dissolved oxygen pressure.
column6: Recognise exposure despite normal PaO2.

column3: Oxidised ferric haemoglobin cannot bind oxygen effectively and increases affinity of remaining ferrous sites.
column4: Cyanosis and a saturation plateau near the mid-80s may occur despite oxygen.
column5: PaO2 can remain normal because dissolved oxygen is unaffected.
column6: Distinguish dyshemoglobinaemia from primary lung failure.

column3: VWF bridges exposed subendothelial collagen to platelet GPIb, initiating adhesion.
column4: Defects cause mucocutaneous bleeding and impaired primary haemostasis.
column5: GPIIb/IIIa mediates aggregation, not initial adhesion.
column6: Differentiate Bernard–Soulier, vWD and Glanzmann mechanisms.

column3: Activated GPIIb/IIIa binds fibrinogen to link platelets.
column4: Inhibition reduces arterial thrombosis but increases bleeding.
column5: Aggregation is distinct from VWF–GPIb adhesion.
column6: Localise platelet receptor disorders.

column3: Tissue factor with factor VII initiates coagulation and is assessed mainly by PT/INR.
column4: Warfarin prolongs PT early because factor VII has a short half-life.
column5: PT is not a direct platelet-function test.
column6: Interpret isolated prolonged PT.

column3: Factors XII, XI, IX and VIII feed into factor X activation and are assessed mainly by aPTT.
column4: Haemophilia A/B typically prolong aPTT with normal PT.
column5: Factor XII deficiency can prolong aPTT without clinical bleeding.
column6: Interpret mixing studies and pathway defects.

column3: Factors II, VII, IX and X plus proteins C and S require vitamin-K-dependent gamma-carboxylation.
column4: Warfarin initially reduces protein C rapidly, creating transient procoagulant risk.
column5: Factor V is not vitamin K dependent.
column6: Explain bridging and reversal physiology.

column3: Systemic coagulation activation consumes platelets and factors while secondary fibrinolysis produces fibrin-degradation products.
column4: Thrombosis and bleeding can coexist with prolonged clotting times, low fibrinogen and high D-dimer.
column5: A high D-dimer alone is nonspecific.
column6: Integrate consumption and microvascular thrombosis.

column3: Haem breakdown produces unconjugated bilirubin carried on albumin to liver, where it is conjugated and excreted in bile.
column4: Haemolysis raises unconjugated bilirubin; obstruction raises conjugated bilirubin and urine bilirubin.
column5: Unconjugated bilirubin is not water-soluble and is absent from urine.
column6: Differentiate prehepatic, hepatic and obstructive patterns.

column3: Iron is absorbed mainly in duodenum; hepcidin degrades ferroportin and reduces intestinal absorption and macrophage iron release.
column4: Inflammation raises hepcidin, causing low serum iron with preserved/high ferritin.
column5: Ferritin is an acute-phase reactant and may mask deficiency.
column6: Differentiate iron deficiency from anaemia of inflammation.

column3: B12 binds intrinsic factor and is absorbed in terminal ileum; body stores are large.
column4: Pernicious anaemia, gastrectomy or ileal disease causes deficiency and neurological dysfunction.
column5: Folate can correct anaemia while neurological B12 injury progresses.
column6: Trace the absorption pathway and avoid folate-only treatment.

column3: Parietal-cell acid secretion is stimulated by histamine H2, acetylcholine M3 and gastrin CCK-B signalling.
column4: PPIs block the final H/K-ATPase step; H2 blockers reduce histamine signalling.
column5: Somatostatin and prostaglandins inhibit secretion and support mucosal defence.
column6: Explain ulcer drugs and rebound physiology.

column3: Secretin stimulates ductal bicarbonate-rich fluid to neutralise gastric acid in the duodenum.
column4: Acid entering the duodenum is the principal secretin stimulus.
column5: CCK mainly stimulates enzyme-rich acinar secretion and gallbladder contraction.
column6: Distinguish secretin from CCK actions.

column3: Bile salts emulsify fat and are reabsorbed mainly in terminal ileum for return to liver.
column4: Ileal disease can cause bile-acid diarrhoea and, with extensive loss, fat malabsorption.
column5: Bile salts are not digestive enzymes.
column6: Link ileal resection to diarrhoea and deficiency.

column3: Poorly absorbed solute retains water in the lumen; stool volume falls with fasting and stool osmotic gap is high.
column4: Lactose intolerance and osmotic laxatives are examples.
column5: Secretory diarrhoea persists despite fasting and has a lower osmotic gap.
column6: Use fasting response and osmotic gap to classify diarrhoea.

column3: Active electrolyte secretion or impaired absorption drives water loss independent of intake.
column4: It persists during fasting and typically has a low stool osmotic gap.
column5: Large-volume watery diarrhoea is not automatically secretory without context.
column6: Differentiate toxin/hormone-mediated secretion from malabsorption.

column3: Portal hydrostatic pressure, splanchnic vasodilation and renal sodium retention promote ascites.
column4: Reduced effective arterial volume activates RAAS, sympathetic tone and ADH despite total-body fluid excess.
column5: Low albumin contributes but does not alone explain portal-hypertensive physiology.
column6: Explain oedema with avid renal sodium retention.

column3: Capillary hydrostatic pressure favours filtration while plasma oncotic pressure favours reabsorption; lymphatics return excess interstitial fluid.
column4: Heart failure, hypoalbuminaemia, permeability injury and lymphatic obstruction produce oedema by different mechanisms.
column5: Modern microvascular physiology emphasises the endothelial glycocalyx and lymphatic return rather than sustained venous-end reabsorption.
column6: Classify oedema mechanism from the clinical setting.

column3: Impaired hepatic detoxification and portosystemic shunting allow ammonia and other neurotoxins to alter astrocyte and neurotransmitter function.
column4: Precipitants include bleeding, infection, constipation, electrolyte disturbance and sedatives.
column5: Serum ammonia supports but does not reliably grade clinical severity.
column6: Identify reversible precipitants rather than treating a number alone.

column3: CPP = mean arterial pressure − intracranial pressure.
column4: A rise in ICP or fall in MAP reduces cerebral perfusion and risks ischaemia.
column5: Maintaining blood pressure without controlling extreme ICP may still leave CPP inadequate.
column6: Interpret head injury and raised-ICP physiology.

column3: Cerebral arterioles normally adjust resistance to stabilise flow across a range of perfusion pressures.
column4: Severe hypertension, hypotension, traumatic injury or stroke can exceed or disrupt autoregulation.
column5: Chronic hypertension shifts the autoregulatory curve rightward.
column6: Explain harm from overly rapid blood-pressure reduction.

column3: Severe raised ICP reduces CPP, provoking sympathetic hypertension; baroreflex bradycardia and abnormal respiration follow.
column4: The triad is a late sign of intracranial decompensation.
column5: It is not a reassuring compensatory state.
column6: Recognise impending herniation.

column3: CSF flows lateral ventricles → foramina of Monro → third ventricle → aqueduct → fourth ventricle → foramina of Luschka/Magendie → subarachnoid space → arachnoid granulations.
column4: Aqueduct obstruction causes non-communicating hydrocephalus with enlarged lateral and third ventricles.
column5: Communicating hydrocephalus occurs distal to ventricular outlets or through impaired absorption.
column6: Localise obstruction from ventricular enlargement.

column3: Total anterior circulation syndrome requires all three: contralateral motor/sensory deficit affecting at least two of face/arm/leg, homonymous visual-field defect, and higher cortical dysfunction.
column4: Usually reflects a large anterior-circulation infarct and carries high risk of oedema and disability.
column5: If only two components are present, classify as PACS rather than TACS.
column6: Apply the Oxford/Bamford clinical classification.

column3: Partial anterior circulation syndrome has two of the three TACS components, or isolated higher cortical dysfunction, or a restricted motor/sensory deficit beyond a classic lacunar pattern.
column4: Often reflects a smaller cortical anterior-circulation infarct.
column5: Pure motor or pure sensory syndromes without cortical signs are usually lacunar.
column6: Separate partial cortical from lacunar stroke.

column3: Lacunar syndrome is a classic small-vessel pattern without cortical dysfunction or visual-field loss: pure motor, pure sensory, sensorimotor, ataxic hemiparesis or dysarthria–clumsy hand.
column4: Typically reflects a small deep perforator infarct.
column5: Aphasia, neglect or homonymous hemianopia argues against LACS.
column6: Recognise deep small-vessel stroke from negative cortical signs.

column3: Posterior circulation syndrome includes brainstem/cerebellar signs, isolated homonymous visual-field loss, or bilateral motor/sensory deficits compatible with vertebrobasilar disease.
column4: May present with diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia, ataxia or crossed findings.
column5: Dizziness alone is nonspecific and does not establish POCS.
column6: Apply posterior-circulation localisation.

column3: Superior-division MCA infarction affects lateral frontal cortex, producing contralateral face/arm weakness and, in the dominant hemisphere, Broca aphasia.
column4: Leg weakness is usually less prominent than face/arm weakness.
column5: Receptive aphasia points more toward inferior division.
column6: Localise cortical deficit and arterial division.

column3: Inferior-division MCA infarction affects lateral temporal/parietal cortex, causing Wernicke aphasia in the dominant hemisphere or neglect in the non-dominant hemisphere.
column4: Motor weakness may be relatively mild compared with cortical deficits.
column5: Fluent speech does not mean language is intact.
column6: Distinguish receptive language/neglect syndromes from motor-predominant stroke.

column3: Proximal MCA occlusion combines cortical dysfunction with dense contralateral face/arm-predominant weakness, sensory loss and visual-field deficit.
column4: Gaze may deviate toward the injured hemisphere acutely.
column5: A pure motor syndrome without cortical signs suggests a deep lacune rather than full MCA stem occlusion.
column6: Recognise a large-vessel thrombectomy phenotype.

column3: ACA supplies medial frontal/parietal cortex; infarction causes contralateral leg-predominant weakness/sensory loss, abulia and sometimes urinary incontinence.
column4: Grasp reflex or behavioural change may occur.
column5: Face/arm-predominant deficits are more typical of MCA territory.
column6: Use somatotopic distribution to localise the artery.

column3: PCA supplies occipital cortex and inferomedial temporal structures; infarction classically causes contralateral homonymous hemianopia.
column4: Dominant-side lesions may cause alexia without agraphia; thalamic involvement can cause sensory syndromes.
column5: Macular sparing may occur but is not obligatory.
column6: Link visual and memory findings to posterior circulation.

column3: The dominant hemisphere, usually left, supports language and learned symbolic functions.
column4: Injury can cause aphasia, apraxia and right–left disorientation.
column5: Dysarthria is a motor-speech problem and is not equivalent to aphasia.
column6: Distinguish language-network dysfunction from articulation weakness.

column3: The non-dominant hemisphere is important for spatial attention and global visuospatial processing.
column4: Injury can cause contralateral neglect, anosognosia and constructional difficulty.
column5: A patient may have intact primary sensation but ignore one side.
column6: Recognise neglect rather than simple sensory loss.

column3: A small infarct in posterior limb internal capsule, basis pontis or corona radiata can affect corticospinal/corticobulbar fibres.
column4: Produces contralateral face, arm and leg weakness without cortical signs.
column5: No aphasia, neglect or visual-field defect should be present.
column6: Localise a deep perforator lesion.

column3: A small thalamic infarct, classically ventral posterolateral/medial nuclei, causes contralateral hemisensory loss.
column4: Motor function and cortical processing are relatively preserved.
column5: Cortical sensory deficits such as extinction suggest cortical disease instead.
column6: Recognise a thalamic lacune.

column3: A deep infarct affecting adjacent sensory and motor pathways causes contralateral weakness plus sensory loss without cortical signs.
column4: Internal capsule/thalamocapsular regions are typical.
column5: Homonymous hemianopia or aphasia should trigger reconsideration.
column6: Distinguish deep combined deficit from large cortical stroke.

column3: A lacune affecting corticospinal and corticopontocerebellar pathways causes ipsilateral-to-weakness limb ataxia with pyramidal weakness.
column4: Common sites include pons or internal capsule.
column5: The ataxia is disproportionate but accompanies weakness on the same body side.
column6: Recognise a classic lacunar syndrome.

column3: A small pontine or internal-capsule lacune causes dysarthria, facial weakness and impaired fine hand movement.
column4: There is no aphasia or major sensory deficit.
column5: Do not mistake slurred speech alone for a cortical language deficit.
column6: Localise a small deep motor pathway lesion.

column3: PICA/vertebral territory injury affects nucleus ambiguus, vestibular nuclei, spinothalamic tract, sympathetic fibres and inferior cerebellar connections.
column4: Dysphagia/hoarseness, ipsilateral facial pain-temperature loss, contralateral body pain-temperature loss, Horner syndrome and ataxia occur.
column5: Prominent limb weakness is usually not the defining feature because corticospinal tract may be spared.
column6: Recognise crossed brainstem signs.

column3: AICA territory injury affects lateral pons and may involve facial nucleus, vestibulocochlear structures and middle cerebellar peduncle.
column4: Facial paralysis, reduced lacrimation/salivation, hearing loss, vertigo and ataxia favour AICA.
column5: Hearing loss and facial weakness distinguish it from classic PICA syndrome.
column6: Use cranial-nerve pattern to localise arterial territory.

column3: Basilar occlusion can damage ventral pons and corticospinal/corticobulbar pathways bilaterally.
column4: Locked-in syndrome produces quadriplegia and anarthria with preserved consciousness and vertical eye movements.
column5: Coma implies reticular activating-system involvement; classic locked-in patients are awake.
column6: Recognise a time-critical posterior large-vessel occlusion.

column3: Brainstem lesions can affect an ipsilateral cranial nerve nucleus/fascicle and a contralateral long tract before or after decussation.
column4: Ipsilateral facial/ocular deficit with contralateral limb deficit localises to brainstem.
column5: Cortical strokes generally produce face and body deficits on the same contralateral side.
column6: Use crossing anatomy to localise lesions.

column3: Border-zone regions between major arterial territories are vulnerable to global hypoperfusion or severe proximal stenosis.
column4: Bilateral ACA–MCA watershed injury can cause proximal arm weakness (“man-in-the-barrel”).
column5: The pattern reflects perfusion failure rather than one distal branch occlusion.
column6: Connect hypotension with border-zone deficits.

column3: The posterior limb is supplied mainly by lenticulostriate branches of MCA and anterior choroidal artery; compact fibres make small lesions clinically dense.
column4: A lacune may affect face, arm and leg together.
column5: Equal dense deficits do not require a large cortical infarct.
column6: Explain disproportionate severity from a small deep lesion.

column3: Anterior choroidal infarction can produce the triad of contralateral hemiplegia, hemisensory loss and homonymous hemianopia.
column4: It affects posterior internal capsule, optic tract/radiations and adjacent structures.
column5: The complete triad is not always present.
column6: Recognise a compact deep arterial territory.

column3: Thalamic relay injury can produce contralateral sensory loss followed by dysaesthesia or central post-stroke pain.
column4: Motor strength may be preserved unless adjacent capsule is involved.
column5: Cortical neglect/extinction is not the same as primary thalamic sensory loss.
column6: Localise pure sensory stroke and delayed pain syndrome.

column3: Cerebellar hemisphere lesions cause ipsilateral limb ataxia because cerebellar output pathways effectively cross twice.
column4: Dysmetria, intention tremor and dysdiadochokinesia occur without primary weakness.
column5: Sensory ataxia worsens markedly with eye closure; cerebellar ataxia persists.
column6: Differentiate cerebellar from sensory ataxia.

column3: Most corticospinal fibres cross in the caudal medullary pyramids.
column4: Lesions above the decussation cause contralateral weakness; spinal lesions below it cause ipsilateral weakness.
column5: Cranial motor patterns depend on corticobulbar projections and are not uniformly bilateral.
column6: Localise brain versus spinal cord lesions.

column3: Fine touch, vibration and proprioception ascend ipsilaterally in spinal cord and cross in the medulla.
column4: A hemicord lesion causes ipsilateral loss below the lesion.
column5: This contrasts with spinothalamic fibres crossing near entry level.
column6: Use sensory modality and side to localise spinal disease.

column3: Pain and temperature fibres synapse and cross within one to two spinal segments through the anterior white commissure.
column4: A unilateral cord lesion causes contralateral loss beginning slightly below the lesion.
column5: Central cord lesions can cause bilateral segmental pain-temperature loss.
column6: Recognise Brown–Séquard and syringomyelia patterns.

column1: Upper versus lower motor neuron physiology
column3: UMN lesions remove descending inhibition, causing spasticity, hyperreflexia and extensor plantar response; LMN lesions cause denervation weakness, wasting, fasciculation and reduced reflexes.
column4: Acute UMN lesions may initially be flaccid.
column5: Tone and reflexes evolve, so timing matters.
column6: Distinguish central from peripheral motor lesions.

column3: End-plate potentials normally exceed threshold by a safety margin; reduced acetylcholine receptors or impaired release makes transmission fail with activity.
column4: Myasthenia causes fatigable weakness; presynaptic facilitation can occur in Lambert–Eaton syndrome.
column5: Sensation remains normal because the lesion is motor transmission.
column6: Explain fatigability and repetitive-stimulation patterns.

column3: Muscle spindles sense length/stretch through Ia afferents and drive monosynaptic alpha-motor-neuron activation.
column4: Loss of descending inhibition exaggerates reflexes in UMN lesions.
column5: Golgi tendon organs sense tension through Ib afferents and are distinct.
column6: Interpret tendon reflexes and tone.
